text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Tense vs. Lax}} is defined as: Tense vowels (e.g., /iː/, /uː/) are longer and more peripheral than lax vowels (e.g., /ɪ/, /ʊ/).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Tense vs. Lax
Description: Tense vowels (e.g., /iː/, /uː/) are longer and more peripheral than lax vowels (e.g., /ɪ/, /ʊ/).
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Tense vs. Lax' es: Tense vowels (e.g., /iː/, /uː/) are longer and more peripheral than lax vowels (e.g., /ɪ/, /ʊ/).

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Schwa /ə/}} is defined as: The most frequent sound in English, occurring in almost all unstressed syllables.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: The Schwa /ə/
Description: The most frequent sound in English, occurring in almost all unstressed syllables.
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'The Schwa /ə/' es: The most frequent sound in English, occurring in almost all unstressed syllables.

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Rhotic Vowels}} is defined as: In GA, vowels are often "r-colored" (e.g., /ɚ/, /ɝ/).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Rhotic Vowels
Description: In GA, vowels are often "r-colored" (e.g., /ɚ/, /ɝ/).
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Rhotic Vowels' es: In GA, vowels are often "r-colored" (e.g., /ɚ/, /ɝ/).

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Aspiration}} is defined as: Voiceless stops /p, t, k/ are aspirated ([pʰ, tʰ, kʰ]) when they occur at the beginning of a stressed syllable, unless preceded by /s/.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Aspiration
Description: Voiceless stops /p, t, k/ are aspirated ([pʰ, tʰ, kʰ]) when they occur at the beginning of a stressed syllable, unless preceded by /s/.
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Aspiration' es: Voiceless stops /p, t, k/ are aspirated ([pʰ, tʰ, kʰ]) when they occur at the beginning of a stressed syllable, unless preceded by /s/.

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Rhoticity}} is defined as: - **Rhotic (GA)**: /r/ is pronounced in all positions.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Rhoticity
Description: - **Rhotic (GA)**: /r/ is pronounced in all positions.
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Rhoticity' es: - **Rhotic (GA)**: /r/ is pronounced in all positions.

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Non-rhotic (RP)}} is defined as: /r/ is only pronounced before a vowel.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Non-rhotic (RP)
Description: /r/ is only pronounced before a vowel.
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Non-rhotic (RP)' es: /r/ is only pronounced before a vowel.

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::L-Vocalization}} is defined as: /l/ has two main allophones: "Clear L" [l] before vowels and "Dark L" [ɫ] in coda positions.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: L-Vocalization
Description: /l/ has two main allophones: "Clear L" [l] before vowels and "Dark L" [ɫ] in coda positions.
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'L-Vocalization' es: /l/ has two main allophones: "Clear L" [l] before vowels and "Dark L" [ɫ] in coda positions.

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Flapping}} is defined as: In GA, /t/ and /d/ become a voiced flap [ɾ] between vowels when the second vowel is unstressed (e.g., *water*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Flapping
Description: In GA, /t/ and /d/ become a voiced flap [ɾ] between vowels when the second vowel is unstressed (e.g., *water*).
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Flapping' es: In GA, /t/ and /d/ become a voiced flap [ɾ] between vowels when the second vowel is unstressed (e.g., *water*).

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Elision (Omission)}} is defined as: Deletion of sounds in fast speech.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Elision (Omission)
Description: Deletion of sounds in fast speech.
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Elision (Omission)' es: Deletion of sounds in fast speech.

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::T-Omission}} is defined as: /t/ is often dropped after /n/ in fast speech (e.g., *interview* -> "innerview" [ˈɪnərvjuː], *wanted* -> "wanned" [ˈwɑnɪd]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: T-Omission
Description: /t/ is often dropped after /n/ in fast speech (e.g., *interview* -> "innerview" [ˈɪnərvjuː], *wanted* -> "wanned" [ˈwɑnɪd]).
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'T-Omission' es: /t/ is often dropped after /n/ in fast speech (e.g., *interview* -> "innerview" [ˈɪnərvjuː], *wanted* -> "wanned" [ˈwɑnɪd]).

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::D-Omission}} is defined as: /d/ is often dropped in consonant clusters (e.g., *you and me* -> "you an' me" [juː ən miː], *stand there* -> "stan' there").
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: D-Omission
Description: /d/ is often dropped in consonant clusters (e.g., *you and me* -> "you an' me" [juː ən miː], *stand there* -> "stan' there").
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'D-Omission' es: /d/ is often dropped in consonant clusters (e.g., *you and me* -> "you an' me" [juː ən miː], *stand there* -> "stan' there").

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::H-Dropping}} is defined as: The initial /h/ of unstressed pronouns (*he, him, her, his*) is often dropped in normal conversation (e.g., *Tell him* -> "tell 'im" [tɛl ɪm], *What did he say?* -> "what did 'e say?" [wʌt dɪd i sɛj]).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: H-Dropping
Description: The initial /h/ of unstressed pronouns (*he, him, her, his*) is often dropped in normal conversation (e.g., *Tell him* -> "tell 'im" [tɛl ɪm], *What did he say?* -> "what did 'e say?" [wʌt dɪd i sɛj]).
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'H-Dropping' es: The initial /h/ of unstressed pronouns (*he, him, her, his*) is often dropped in normal conversation (e.g., *Tell him* -> "tell 'im" [tɛl ɪm], *What did he say?* -> "what did 'e say?" [wʌt dɪd i sɛj]).

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Assimilation (Sound Substitution)}} is defined as: Sounds changing to match neighboring sounds.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Assimilation (Sound Substitution)
Description: Sounds changing to match neighboring sounds.
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Assimilation (Sound Substitution)' es: Sounds changing to match neighboring sounds.

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::N-to-M / N-to-NG}} is defined as: e.g., *ten mice* -> /tɛm maɪs/, *one cup* -> /wʌŋ kʌp/.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: N-to-M / N-to-NG
Description: e.g., *ten mice* -> /tɛm maɪs/, *one cup* -> /wʌŋ kʌp/.
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'N-to-M / N-to-NG' es: e.g., *ten mice* -> /tɛm maɪs/, *one cup* -> /wʌŋ kʌp/.

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Yod-Coalescence}} is defined as: When /t/ or /d/ meets /j/ (y) in connected speech, they merge:
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Yod-Coalescence
Description: When /t/ or /d/ meets /j/ (y) in connected speech, they merge:
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Yod-Coalescence' es: When /t/ or /d/ meets /j/ (y) in connected speech, they merge:

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Linking & Liaison}} is defined as: Fusing a final consonant with an initial vowel (e.g., *an apple* -> /əˈnæpəl/, *not at all* -> /ˌnɒtəˈtɔːl/).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Linking & Liaison
Description: Fusing a final consonant with an initial vowel (e.g., *an apple* -> /əˈnæpəl/, *not at all* -> /ˌnɒtəˈtɔːl/).
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Linking & Liaison' es: Fusing a final consonant with an initial vowel (e.g., *an apple* -> /əˈnæpəl/, *not at all* -> /ˌnɒtəˈtɔːl/).

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Intrusion}} is defined as: Inserting a transitional consonant between adjacent vowels:
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Intrusion
Description: Inserting a transitional consonant between adjacent vowels:
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Intrusion' es: Inserting a transitional consonant between adjacent vowels:

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::/w/ Intrusion}} is defined as: After close-back vowels /uː, oʊ, aʊ/ (e.g., *go out* -> /ɡoʊ ʷ aʊt/).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: /w/ Intrusion
Description: After close-back vowels /uː, oʊ, aʊ/ (e.g., *go out* -> /ɡoʊ ʷ aʊt/).
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de '/w/ Intrusion' es: After close-back vowels /uː, oʊ, aʊ/ (e.g., *go out* -> /ɡoʊ ʷ aʊt/).

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::/j/ Intrusion}} is defined as: After close-front vowels /iː, eɪ, aɪ/ (e.g., *pay all* -> /peɪ ʲ ɔːl/).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: /j/ Intrusion
Description: After close-front vowels /iː, eɪ, aɪ/ (e.g., *pay all* -> /peɪ ʲ ɔːl/).
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de '/j/ Intrusion' es: After close-front vowels /iː, eɪ, aɪ/ (e.g., *pay all* -> /peɪ ʲ ɔːl/).

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::/r/ Intrusion (RP/Non-rhotic)}} is defined as: Inserting /r/ between final and initial vowels (e.g., *law and order* -> /ˈlɔːrəndˌɔːdə/).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: /r/ Intrusion (RP/Non-rhotic)
Description: Inserting /r/ between final and initial vowels (e.g., *law and order* -> /ˈlɔːrəndˌɔːdə/).
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de '/r/ Intrusion (RP/Non-rhotic)' es: Inserting /r/ between final and initial vowels (e.g., *law and order* -> /ˈlɔːrəndˌɔːdə/).

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Vowel Reduction}} is defined as: Unstressed grammatical/function words undergo extreme reduction to the schwa /ə/ or near-close vowels:
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Vowel Reduction
Description: Unstressed grammatical/function words undergo extreme reduction to the schwa /ə/ or near-close vowels:
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Vowel Reduction' es: Unstressed grammatical/function words undergo extreme reduction to the schwa /ə/ or near-close vowels:

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Word Stress}} is defined as: English is a **stress-timed** language. Primary stress is phonemic (e.g., *RE-cord* (noun) vs. *re-CORD* (verb)).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Word Stress
Description: English is a **stress-timed** language. Primary stress is phonemic (e.g., *RE-cord* (noun) vs. *re-CORD* (verb)).
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Word Stress' es: English is a **stress-timed** language. Primary stress is phonemic (e.g., *RE-cord* (noun) vs. *re-CORD* (verb)).

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Sentence Stress}} is defined as: Content words (nouns, verbs, adjectives) are usually stressed, while function words (prepositions, articles) are reduced.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Sentence Stress
Description: Content words (nouns, verbs, adjectives) are usually stressed, while function words (prepositions, articles) are reduced.
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Sentence Stress' es: Content words (nouns, verbs, adjectives) are usually stressed, while function words (prepositions, articles) are reduced.

text: In English phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Intonation}} is defined as: Pitch rises for questions and falls for statements, though this varies by dialect.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for English.
usage:
Rule: Intonation
Description: Pitch rises for questions and falls for statements, though this varies by dialect.
spanish: En la fonética de English, la regla de 'Intonation' es: Pitch rises for questions and falls for statements, though this varies by dialect.

text: What is the English pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Connected Speech Pro-Tip}}? To sound natural, do not pronounce every word separately. Link final consonants to initial vowels, drop your 'h' sounds in unstressed pronouns like 'him' or 'her', and reduce small grammar words like 'can' and 'to' to a short, quick schwa 'ə'.
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for English.
usage:
Tip: Connected Speech Pro-Tip
To sound natural, do not pronounce every word separately. Link final consonants to initial vowels, drop your 'h' sounds in unstressed pronouns like 'him' or 'her', and reduce small grammar words like 'can' and 'to' to a short, quick schwa 'ə'.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de English para 'Connected Speech Pro-Tip'? To sound natural, do not pronounce every word separately. Link final consonants to initial vowels, drop your 'h' sounds in unstressed pronouns like 'him' or 'her', and reduce small grammar words like 'can' and 'to' to a short, quick schwa 'ə'.

text: What is the English pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The "Wanned" Effect}}? In American English, when 't' comes after 'n' and is followed by an unstressed vowel, it is almost always omitted in fast speech. Try saying "plen-ty", "twen-ty", and "in-ter-net" without the 't' sound!
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for English.
usage:
Tip: The "Wanned" Effect
In American English, when 't' comes after 'n' and is followed by an unstressed vowel, it is almost always omitted in fast speech. Try saying "plen-ty", "twen-ty", and "in-ter-net" without the 't' sound!
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de English para 'The "Wanned" Effect'? In American English, when 't' comes after 'n' and is followed by an unstressed vowel, it is almost always omitted in fast speech. Try saying "plen-ty", "twen-ty", and "in-ter-net" without the 't' sound!

text: What is the English pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Tip}}? Use the `ə` symbol often; it is the key to natural-sounding English rhythm.
explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for English.
usage:
Tip: Tip
Use the `ə` symbol often; it is the key to natural-sounding English rhythm.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de English para 'Tip'? Use the `ə` symbol often; it is the key to natural-sounding English rhythm.
